coredata.dc:title: Dual-emission ratio fluorescent probe based on carbon dots and copper-silver bimetallic nanoclusters for visual and fluorescent detection of ciprofloxacin
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kang, Chenyue
Zhang, Caihong
Zhang, Yan
Zhang, Guomei
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: Ciprofloxacin (CIP) plays an important role in many physiological processes. Previously reported methods for CIP detection have many drawbacks and so it is crucial to develop highly sensitive methods to detect CIP. In this study, a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe (SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs) of a nanocomposite of carbon quantum dots (SF-CQDs) and copper-silver bimetallic nanoclusters (CuAg NCs) was developed for sensitive and selective detection of CIP. The fluorescent signal of CuAg NCs was quenched while the fluorescence intensity of SF-CQDs was enhanced in the presence of CIP. The working principle of the fluorescent probe SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs is mainly based on the complexation of CIP with CuAg NCs to free SF-CQDs. The quenching of CuAg NCs by CIP arises from the chelation between the metal and the 1-oxo substituent and the adjacent carboxyl group of CIP. The fluorescence recovery of SF-CQDs is attributing to the release of SF-CQDs from SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs and the hydrogen bonding interaction between SF-CQDs and CIP. The ratiometric fluorescence measurement of SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs has been employed to analyze CIP with good sensitivity and selectivity. The working range of CIP is 5.0–200.0 μM and the detection limit is 3.3 μM. More importantly, the fluorescence of SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs can be detected intuitively via a smartphone which can be applied to determine CIP.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent probes
Copper-silver bimetallic nanoclusters
Carbon quantum dots
CIP detection
Smartphone RGB analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of CIP and real sample analysis
Characterization of SF-CQDs, CEW@CuAg NCs and SF-CQDs/CuAg NCS
Optical properties of SF-CQDs, CEW@CuAg NCs and SF-CQDs/CuAg NCS
Interaction between SF-CQDs and CEW@CuAg NCs
Interaction between CIP and SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs
Visualization detection of CIP by smartphone RGB

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images、HRTEM images and histograms of particle size distribution of SF-CQDs (a), CEW@CuAg NCs (b), and SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs (c).

(a) IR spectra of SF-CQDs, CEW@CuAg NCs and SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs. XPS of SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs: (b) survey scan, (c) high-resolution spectrum of Cu2p, (d) high-resolution spectrum of Ag3d. (e) UV–vis absorption (Abs), excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs. The insets show pictures of SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs under sunlight (left) and UV light (right). (f) Zeta potentials of SF-CQDs, CEW@CuAg NCs and SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs at various NaCl concentrations. (b) Effect of pH on the fluorescence emission of SF-CQDs and CEW@CuAg NCs. (c) Emission spectra of SF-CQDs, CEW@AgCu NCs and SF-CQDs/AgCu NCs at excitation 330 nm. (d) Time-resolved spectra of SF-CQDs and SF-CQDs/AgCu NCs at Ex 330/Em 440 nm. (e) Emission spectra of SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs with various concentrations of CIP (0.0–200.0 μM). The inset displays the images of SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs with various concentrations of CIP under UV light. The concentration of CIP increases from0.0 μM to 200.0 μM. The other inset displays ratiometric F600 /F440 calibration curve of CIP (5.0–200.0 μM) using SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs. (f) F600 /F440 of SF-CQDS/CuAg NCs probe in the presence of various potential interferents and CIP.

(a) Schematic of CIP detection using a smartphone. (b) The color change of fluorescent strips at 365 nm UV light. (c) The linear relationship between the R/B of SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs solutions at different concentrations of CIP.
The synthesis and detection of CIP by SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs.
Comparison of different sensors for the detection of CIP.
Recovery of CIP determination in real samples.

head.ce:title: Dual-emission ratio fluorescent probe based on carbon dots and copper-silver bimetallic nanoclusters for visual and fluorescent detection of ciprofloxacin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ciprofloxacin (CIP) plays an important role in many physiological processes. Previously reported methods for CIP detection have many drawbacks and so it is crucial to develop highly sensitive methods to detect CIP. In this study, a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe (SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs) of a nanocomposite of carbon quantum dots (SF-CQDs) and copper-silver bimetallic nanoclusters (CuAg NCs) was developed for sensitive and selective detection of CIP. The fluorescent signal of CuAg NCs was quenched while the fluorescence intensity of SF-CQDs was enhanced in the presence of CIP. The working principle of the fluorescent probe SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs is mainly based on the complexation of CIP with CuAg NCs to free SF-CQDs. The quenching of CuAg NCs by CIP arises from the chelation between the metal and the 1-oxo substituent and the adjacent carboxyl group of CIP. The fluorescence recovery of SF-CQDs is attributing to the release of SF-CQDs from SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs and the hydrogen bonding interaction between SF-CQDs and CIP. The ratiometric fluorescence measurement of SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs has been employed to analyze CIP with good sensitivity and selectivity. The working range of CIP is 5.0–200.0 μM and the detection limit is 3.3 μM. More importantly, the fluorescence of SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs can be detected intuitively via a smartphone which can be applied to determine CIP.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ciprofloxacin (CIP), a second-generation fluoroquinolone broad-acting antibiotic [1], was first discovered in 1987 as a new quinoline carboxylic acid derivative with higher antimicrobial activity than earlier related compound [2]. CIP can often be used in the treatment of infections against a wide range of pathogenic bacteria. Currently, studies have shown that the therapeutic range of CIP includes bone infections, joint infections, abdominal infections, as well as specific types of infectious gastroenteritis, respiratory tract infections, skin infections, typhoid fever, and urinary tract infection [3]. However, the heavy use and misuse of CIP have resulted in severe residues in foods of animal origin, which can lead to adverse reactions such as poor metabolism and slow degradation when it enters the human body [4]. Therefore, the detection of CIP has also become very important nowadays. In nature, many fruit growers use antibiotics to resist the effects of bacterial outbreaks on honey because of the susceptibility of honeybee colonies to bacterial diseases, and the most indispensable in the growth and development of both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria is the enzyme DNA gyrase, which is an essential topoisomerase involved in the process of bacterial replication [5]. CIP mainly acts as an inhibitor of DNA gyrase [6], and therefore, many beekeepers use CIP, an antibiotic, to inhibit bacterial infections in honey during the production process. However, large amounts of CIP residues in honey can have adverse effects on human health. As such, it is essential to monitor the CIP residues in honey for food safety and health reasons. However, since honey contains a very large number of complex compositional matrices, this poses a great challenge to analyze the undesirable matrices in honey. Currently, there are many methods to detect antibiotics in food samples, such as capillary electrophoresis [7], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [8], and LC-MS [9] are being widely used for the separation and detection of antibiotics and their residues in food samples. However, it is often difficult to analyze the antibiotic residues because they are found to be associated with matrices that interfere with their detection. Moreover, these analytical techniques often require expensive equipment and cumbersome sample pretreatment processes; as a result, the development of simple and cost-effective analytical methods is of great value in analysis and detection of antibiotics.

Fluorescence analysis is often used for various assays due to their advantages of fast response, simple operation, high sensitivity, and ease of visual inspection, and among them, single-channel fluorescence analysis has been widely used [10–12]. Unfortunately, single-channel fluorescence analysis is susceptible to the concentration of sensing materials and the drift of the incident light which greatly limits their accurate detection. Thus, to avoid these drawbacks, ratiometric fluorescence measurements based on dual fluorescence emissions have been developed [13–16]. Ratiometric fluorescence methods can record two or more independent fluorescence signals during the analysis, which can effectively improve the signal-to-noise ratio and achieve a reliable quantitative analysis of the analysis, which not only ensures that the detectors have the widest visual color range but also effectively improves the detection sensitivity of the detectors and achieves reliable quantitative analysis [17–21].

To date, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely used in the biological field because of their good photoluminescence stability, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility [22]. So, CQDs are often used in various fields such as fluorescent sensing, bioimaging, and drug delivery [23,24]. In fluorescent sensing, CQDs are mainly used for the detection of heavy metal ions. Using the principle of fluorescence burst, they have been used to detect iron ions, silver ions, and some biomolecules [25,26]. As a natural protein, silk protein has a wide range of applications in various fields due to its unique advantages of green economy, good biocompatibility and excellent mechanical properties. However, the use of SF as a carbon source material is rare. Furthermore, metal nanoclusters (NCs) have the advantages of large Stokes shift, good biocompatibility, long fluorescence lifetime, and easy conjugation [27]. Various gold (Au), silver (Ag) [28], and copper (Cu) nanoclusters [29] have been synthesized with different stabilizers over the past few years. While single metal NCs have been extensive studied, the use of bimetallic NCs is not much reported.

Ovalbumin (OVA) is a typical glycoprotein derived from chicken egg white (CEW) which is enriched with sulfhydryl, amino, and carboxyl functional groups. OVA as a template for syntheses of Au, Ag and Cu NCs have been reported. Zhang et al. synthesized a red-lighting Ag NCs using OVA as the template and applied it as a Hg fluorescent “turn-off” sensor [2,30]. He et al. synthesized four colors (green, yellow, orange, and red) of Cu NCs and employed it for detection of ethanol (EtOH) in liquor [31].

Fluorescence measurements were conducted on a Hitachi F-4500 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan) with a quartz cuvette (1 cm × 1 cm). UV–visible spectra were recorded on a Hitachi UV-2910 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence lifetimes were captured by an Edinburgh FFLS-920 spectrofluorometer (Livingstone, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on a Hitachi Kratos Axis Ultradld x-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Tokyo Japan). Zeta potentials were measured on a Malvern ZEN3690 Zetasizer (Westborough, MA, US). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (FEI, Tokyo, Japan). The infrared measurements were collected with a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Waltham, MA, US).

Copper(II) sulfate, pentahydrate (CuSO4⋅5H2O, 99 %), silver nitrate (AgNO3 > 99 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH > 96 %), calcium chloride, and EtOH were obtain from Beijing Chemical Plant (Beijing, China). Hydroxylamine hydrochloride (NH2OH·HCl), ascorbic Acid (AA), D,L-phenylaianine (PHE), D,L-methionine (MET), alanine (ALA) were brought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Diphenylamine (DPA), vitamin H, vitamin B2, vitamin B3, vitamin B6, vitamin C, glucose, cysteine (CYS), sulfadiazine (SD), gentamycin (GM), lysozyme (LYS), dopamine (DA), erythromycin (EM), and CIP were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Beijing, China). All reagents and other chemicals were of at least analytical grade. Silk worms, eggs and honey were from the local supermarket. Ultrapure water was used throughout for the studies. All solutions were freshly prepared in water under ambient conditions.

The synthesis of SF-CQDs was based on a previous report [33] with some slight modifications. First, silk worms were degummed with Na2CO3 solution and then kept in an aqueous solution for 2 h at 100 °C. Second, the clean silk worms were dissolved in a solution with CaCl2/H2O/EtOH molar ratio of 1:8:2 for 2 h at 100 °C to obtain the crude SF solution which was further purified by dialysis and filtration. Finally, the clean SF solution was transferred into an autoclave and heated at 190 °C for 3 h to obtain the SF-CQDS product. Third, the crude SF-CQDS was transferred to the dialysisbag (MW 1000D) for 48 h to remove the unreacted substance. Finally, SF-CQDS in dialysis bag were freeze-dried to obtain the yellow solid and put this solid into a refrigerator at 4 °C for subsequent use.

2.0 mL CuSO4⋅5H2O (10 mM), 1.0 mL AgNO3 (20 mM) and 3 mL CEW were stirred and mixed together for 1 h at room temperature, and followed with 1.5 mL NH2OH·HCl (1.5 M). A small aliquot of 1 M NaOH was then added to adjust the solution to pH 11.0 and stirred for 2 h until the reaction was completed. The solution was transferred to the dialysisbag (MW 1000 D) for dialysis for 24 h to remove the unreacted substance, then freeze-dried to obtain the yellow solid and put this solid into a refrigerator at 4 °C for subsequent use.

The synthesis of SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs was mixing 1 mL SF-CQDs (1 mg/mL) with 2 mL CEW@CuAg NCs(1 mg/mL). Stirring continuously for two hours in the dark. Finally the solution was transferred to dialysisbag (MW 1000D) for dialysis for 24 h to remove the unreacted CEW@CuAg NCs and SF-CQDs, then put the solution into refrigerator 4 °C for future use.

The QYs of SF-CQDs and CEW@CuAg NCs were calculated using quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4 as solvent). The QYs were calculated according to the equation:
QY = Q Y s t
I
Ist
×
Ast
A
×
η
2
η s t
2
× 100 %
, where I and A are the integral area of fluorescence emission peak and absorbance at excitation wavelength, respectively; η is the refractive index of the solvent. The QYs of SF-CQDS (excitation 310 nm) and CEW@CuAg NCS (excitation 330 nm) were determined as 5.9 % and 9.2 %, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of CIP and real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: 1 mL SF-CQDs/Cu AgNCs (1.0 mg/mL) was added to a 5 mL-volumetric test tube containing 4.0 mL of 0.1 M phosphate buffer saline (pH 7.0). 5.0 μL of various concentrations of CIP was injected into the SF-CQDs/Cu AgNCs solution and incubated for 10 min. The ratiometric fluorescence intensities (F600 /F440 ) at excitation 330 nm of SF-CQDs/Cu Ag NCs with CIP was recorded and a calibration curve was constructed by plotting F600 /F440 against concentration of CIP. To demonstrate the feasibility of the developed ratiometric fluorescent probe, CIP in honey was analysed and determined. 1 g honey was diluted with 50 mL ultrapure water and 5.0 μL of the diluted sample was applied to the SF-CQDs/Cu AgNCs solution for F600/F440 measurement. Spiked and non-spiked samples were evaluated for their CIP contents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of SF-CQDs, CEW@CuAg NCs and SF-CQDs/CuAg NCS
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and structure of SF-CQDs, CEW@CuAg NCs and SF-CQDs/CuAg NCS were investigated by TEM. Fig. 1 display the TEM images of SF-CQDS, CEW@CuAg NCS and SF-CQD/CuAg NCs, respectively. SF-CQDs, CEW@CuAg NCs and SF-CQD/CuAg NCs are spherical and well dispersed in aqueous solution. The Gaussian fitting of the histograms obtained by TEM in the insets of Fig. 1a–c indicate that the particle size distributions of SF-CQDs, CEW@CuAg NCs and SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs are in the range 1.40–3.50, 1.50–2.60 and 1.50–4.60 nm with the average particle sizes of 2.50, 2.20 and 3.80 nm, respectively. The particle size of CEW@CuAg NCs is smaller than that of SF-CQDs, SF-CQDs/Cu Ag NCs has the largest particle size as it is composed of SF-CQDs and CEW@CuAg NCs nanoparticles. The inset images in Fig. 1a–c shows that a high-resolution transmission electron microscope produced a high-magnification image that revealed that the SF-CQDs、CEW@CuAg NCs and SF-CQDs/Cu Ag NCs had a lattice spacing of 0.261 nm, 0.255 nm and 0.315 nm.

In order to determine the surface functionality of the nanoparticles, IR spectra of SF-CQDS, CEW@CuAg NCS and SF-CQDs/CuAg NCS were examined and shown in Fig. 1a. The spectrum of SF-CQDS is mainly composed of 3389.33, 1637.03, 1515.37, 1247.10, 1134.41, and 873.26 cm−1, corresponding to OH/NH stretching, CO stretching, CC/CN stretching, CS stretching, CO stretching, and CH out-of-plane bending vibrations. Thus, the surface of SF-CQDs possesses hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, and amide functional groups. The IR spectrum of CEW@CuAg NCs shows that its surface is mainly composed of OH/NH (3417.07 cm−1), CH (2961.86 cm−1), CO (1655.78 cm−1), CC/CN (1544.10 cm−1), and CO (1241.09 cm−1) groups. SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs contains almost all the IR absorption peaks of SF-CQDs and CuAg NCs, indicating that it is consisted of the SF-CQDs and CuAg NCs nanoparticles. Fig. 2 b displays the XPS survey scan spectrum of SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs, indicating that it is composed of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur, copper, and silver. Fig. 2c displays the XPS Cu2p spectrum of SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs which has two peaks at 932.4 and 952.2 eV, assigning to the electronic binding energies of 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 for Cu (0) and Cu (Ⅰ), respectively. These results indicate that SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs is composed of Cu (0) and Cu (Ⅰ). Fig. 2d depicts the XPS Ag spectrum of SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs that it has two peaks at 368.2 and 374.3 eV, corresponding to Ag3d5/2 and Ag3d3/2, respectively. The peaks are approximately the binding energies of Ag (368.0 and 374.0 eV), inferring that Ag(0) is present in the SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs. The C1s and O1s spectra of SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs are depicted in Fig. S1a and b, respectively. The deconvoluted spectrum of C1s is divided into three peaks at 284.62, 285.52 and 288.39 eV, belonging to CC/CC, CN and CO bonds, respectively. The O1s spectrum is deconvoluted into three different peaks at 530.88, 531.68 and 536.76 eV, assigning to COOH, CO and CO/COH/COC groups, respectively. Fig. S1c shows the deconvoluted spectrum of S2p which is divided into two peaks at 162.60 eV (CSC) and 169.18 eV (CSO2 C). Fig. S1d displays the deconvoluted spectrum of N1s which can be divided into two peaks at 399.18 eV (CN) and 400.10 eV (CNC).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of SF-CQDs, CEW@CuAg NCs and SF-CQDs/CuAg NCS
Interaction between SF-CQDs and CEW@CuAg NCs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2f depicts the zeta potentials of SF-CQDs, CEW@CuAg NCs and SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs. All of their zeta potentials are negative, inferring that they are covered with negatively charged functionalities. The zeta potentials of SF-CQDs and SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs are close and much larger than that of CEW@CuAg NCs. The smaller negative zeta potential of CEW@CuAg NCs is probably attributing to the coordination of the negative charged functional groups with some Cu (I) on CEW@CuAg NCs, as illustrated in Fig. 2c. SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs are formed by combining SF-CQDs with CEW@CuAg NCs. SF-CQDs and CEW@CuAg NCs can be assembled by supramolecular weak interaction forces which are van der Waals forces, electrostatic interaction forces to form SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs nanocomposites. The effect of ionic strength on SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs is shown in Fig. 3 a NaCl (0.0–0.5 M) does not cause any change in the fluorescence of SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs, indicating that the ionic strength does not affect the fluorescence intensity of SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs. Fig. 3b depicts the effect of pH on the fluorescence emissions of SF-CQDs and CEW@CuAg NCs. The emission of SF-CQDs and CEW@CuAg NCs increases with the increase in pH and reaches the highest at pH 7. Further increase in pH causes the decrease in emission intensity. Similarly, SF-CQDs/CuAgNCs has the highest intensity at pH 7. Thus, 7 was chosen as the optimal pH for this work. Fig. 3c presents the emission spectra of SF-CQDs, CEW@CuAg NCs and SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs at Ex 330 nm. The emission band at 440 nm of SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs is weaker than that of SF-CQDs, inferring that CEW@CuAg NCs quenches the emission of SF-CQDs in SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs. It is possible that the metal of CuAg NCs binds with the surface functional groups of SF-CQDs to inhibit the emission of SF-CQDs at 440 nm. However, the emission band at 600 nm of SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs remains more or less the same as that of CEW@CuAg NCs, indicating that SF-CQDs in SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs does not affect CEW@CuAg NCs emission. The average fluorescence lifetimes at Ex 330/Em 440 nm of SF-CQDs and SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs in Fig. 3d are 6.55 and 6.50 ns, respectively. The lifetimes are almost unchanged, inferring that CEW@CuAg NCs may cause static quenching on SF-CQDs. In brief, the carbonyl and hydroxyl functionalities of SF-CQDs could bind to the CEW@CuAg NCs (vida infra).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction between CIP and SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs
ce:section.ce:para: The quantitative detection of CIP was evaluated by measuring the fluorescence changes of SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs. Fig. 3e depicts the effect of CIP on the emission spectrum of SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs. The emission at 440 nm gradually increases with the increase in concentration of CIP whereas the emission at 600 nm gradually decreases. It is possible that CIP binds to CEW@CuAg NCs of the SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs nanocomposite to release SF-CQDs; It is well known that there are interactions of metal ions with quinolone antimicrobial agents such as norfloxacin dihydrochloride, lomefloxacin, rosoxacin, sparfloxacin and ciprofloxacin. However interactions between different quinolone drugs and metals showed differences [34,36]. The structure of CIP is shown in Fig. S5a. CIP belongs to the quinolone class of drugs and the mechanism of interaction of nootropics with metal cations is chelation between the metal and the 1-oxo and adjacent carboxyl groups. The effects of different nootropics on metal cations vary, among them CIP molecule contains two ionizable groups, the carboxyl group and the amino group of the piperazine ring, which can be used as an active group for the complexation with metal cations [35]. The mechanism of CIP and CuAg NCs complexation is manifested by the ability of CIP to form strong complexes with multivalent metal ions. The structure is described as a bidentate complex formed between the metal ions and CIP via a ketone O (C-1) and an O of the carboxyl group (C-2) [36]. Moreover, CIP (a norbornone) is an amphoteric molecule so that the degree of ionization of the acidic and basic groups of CIP varies with the pH of the solution, which leads to a difference in the affinity of CIP for metal ions under acidic and basic conditions [37–39]. Turel et al. measured the binding of CIP and copper ions complexation constants at different pH and proposed the formation of several different aqueous substances, where CIP and copper ions formed Cu(CIP)2+ and Cu(CIP)2 2+ stabilized complexes under pH-neutral conditions [40]. Silver ions may have formed a monodentate complex with CIP [41]. Fig. S5b shows a tendency of aggregation of CEW@CuAg NCs after reacting with CIP, inferring the gradual formation of complex between CIP and CEW@CuAg NCs.

In addition, the fluorescence recovery of SF-CQDs is probably attributing to the hydrogen bonding interaction between SF-CQDs and CIP as shown in Fig. S5a. SF-CQDS has a large surface area and is rich in carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, which leads to hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl group of SF-CQDs and the piperazine ring and carboxyl group of CIP, resulting in fluorescence enhancement [42]. Fig. S5a and b display the time-resolved spectra of CEW@CuAg NCs in the absence and presence of CIP. The fluorescence lifetime of CEW@CuAg NCs is 19.33 μs and decreases to 18.05 μs after reacting with CIP, indicating that charge transfer from CIP to CuAg NCs is taken place.

Previous studies have shown that excess CIP in honey can cause harmful effects on the humans. In order to apply our proposed SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs ratiometric fluorescent probe for real samples, commercially available honey samples were purchased for CIP detection. Spiked honey samples were also subjected to the analysis of CIP by the SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs ratiometric fluorescent probe. Table 2 summarizes the recovery of the spiked samples. The recoveries of the honey samples range from 98.35 % to 103.10 % and the relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 5) is within 5 %. Finally, the effects of possible interferences such as vitamin B2, vitamin B3, vitamin B6, vitamin C, and glucose in honey on the ratiometric fluorescence probe were investigated and displayed in Fig. S6. Basically, these interferences do not cause any effect on the detection of CIP. As such, the proposed fluorescence ratio probe has good feasibility for the detection of CIP in honey.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization detection of CIP by smartphone RGB
ce:section.ce:para: The colour of SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs solution under UV light changes from orange-red to blue with increasing CIP concentration. So Fig. 4 a was constructed by combining smartphone RGB analysis with fluorescent test strips to predict different concentrations of CIP. The R & B values of the strip papers are obtained by the color recognition extractor of the smartphone. Fig. 4b displays the plot of R/B against the concentration of CIP. A good linear relationship between R/B and concentration of CIP (0.0–200.0 μM) is observed (R/B = −0.01073 [CIP] + 2.23, r 2 = 0.9954). Fig. 4c shows the colour change of the fluorescent strip at 365 nm with increasing concentration of CIP from 0.0 to 200.0 μM. As such, smartphone RGB analysis combined with the test strips can be applied to determine CIP concentrations.

In essence, a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent platform based on SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs has been developed to determine CIP. The fluorescence of SF-CQDs is reduced by CEW@CuAg NCs but it can be recovered in the presence of CIP. At the same time, CIP binds with CuAg NCs to quench its fluorescence. Our proposed dual-emission SF-CQDs/CuAg NCs probe can be applied for the analysis of CIP in honey. It is also possible to use a smartphone to detect CIP.

Chenyue Kang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Caihong Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation. Yan Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation. Guomei Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shaomin Shuang: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support by Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (N0.202303021221078).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110810.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
